stimulus: Since the 1990s, music critics and broadcasters in Western Europe have grouped a wide variety of musical traditions from East Asia under the umbrella term “Asian fusion.” While some welcome this label for raising the visibility of traditions like Korean pansori and Mongolian throat-singing in European concert programs, musicologist Hyun-Joo Kim argues that the term flattens highly distinct musical lineages by treating them as variants of a single style.
stem: Which finding about pansori and Mongolian throat-singing, if true, would most directly support Kim’s claim?

answerOptions.content: Pansori is more widely performed in European concert halls than Mongolian throat-singing is.
Both traditions are now studied in conservatories across East Asia by students from many backgrounds.
Both pansori and Mongolian throat-singing differ markedly from Western art music but resemble each other in vocal technique.
Pansori and Mongolian throat-singing developed independently of each other and share very few musical features.

rationale: Choice D is the best answer because Kim’s claim is that the umbrella term flattens distinct traditions. Showing that pansori and Mongolian throat-singing developed independently and share little musically directly supports the claim that lumping them together obscures real differences. Choice A is about popularity. Choice B is about pedagogy. Choice C actually undercuts Kim’s claim by implying the two traditions are similar.
